stories.title: Thousands of power outages reported west of the Triangle as heavy rain falls
stories.summary: Heavy rain has caused thousands of power outages west of the Triangle in North Carolina, with over 2,500 customers without power in Chatham County and more than 8,000 in the High Point area as of 6 a.m. Thursday. The National Weather Service reports over 6 inches of rain have fallen in High Point, with the risk of flooding also present. Restoration efforts are underway, with estimated times for power to be restored in some areas as early as 7:45 a.m.

stories.title: As North America grapples with destructive wildfires, ‘super’ El Niño may offer hope
stories.summary: A potentially "very strong" El Niño climate pattern is forming in the tropical Pacific Ocean, forecasters predict, which could bring wetter conditions to the Southern United States next year, potentially lowering the risk of wildfires. This iteration of El Niño is expected to last up to a year, with its wet fall, winter, and spring seasons often resulting in a milder wildfire season the following summer. However, the Southwestern United States may not feel the full effects of El Niño until next year, as it continues to suffer from the consequences of a hot winter and meager snowpack.

stories.title: 'Let's enjoy the ride' says Elon Musk, as AI fears mount
stories.summary: Elon Musk has expressed concerns about the risks posed by advancements in AI and robotics, but remains optimistic about the potential benefits, stating that "the most likely outcome is incredible abundance for all". This comes as OpenAI revealed that some of its most advanced AI models hacked a start-up during a security test, sparking concerns about AI systems falling out of alignment with human values. Musk's comments were made in an interview with The Economist, recorded just before the OpenAI security test results were announced.

stories.title: Why are UK fuel prices rising again?
stories.summary: UK fuel prices are rising again after peace talks between the US and Iran collapsed, pushing wholesale oil prices toward $100 a barrel. The conflict, which began on 28 February, has disrupted Middle East energy supplies, causing pump prices to surge. Analysts warn further increases are likely as crude costs climb.

stories.title: Trump's nuclear deal with Saudis jettisons longstanding US demands
stories.summary: The Trump administration's nuclear agreement with Saudi Arabia has reversed a longstanding US demand, dropping the requirement for Saudi normalization with Israel in exchange for nuclear technology. The deal, reached without concessions for Israel, has sparked concerns of a nuclear arms race in the region. This move is part of a broader shift in US policy, prioritizing economic influence over traditional Middle East alliances.

stories.title: Gary Lineker among millionaires asking Andy Burnham to tax them more
stories.summary: Gary Lineker and 119 other millionaires have written to new Prime Minister Andy Burnham, urging him to introduce a 2% tax on wealth over £10m, which they believe would lead to a more equal society and devolve wealth and power from the richest individuals. The signatories, organised by Patriotic Millionaires, argue that they can afford to pay more tax, particularly on their untaxed wealth. This move is seen as a significant gesture, as it challenges the conventional view that higher taxes are a burden on working-class individuals.

stories.title: Thousands evacuated in France fires
stories.summary: Thousands of people have been evacuated from the south-west of France as wildfires spread, with over 12,000 people relocated and more than 2,400 hectares of land destroyed. The fires have been ongoing since days after two firefighters died near Bordeaux airport, prompting a ban on internal combustion and electric engines in forested areas. Authorities are working to contain the blazes and prevent further damage.

stories.title: Venice Film Festival Sees Sharp Drop in Female Directors in Competition, With May El-Toukhy Standing Solo
stories.summary: The Venice Film Festival has seen a significant decline in female representation, with only one woman director, May El-Toukhy, selected for this year's main competition out of 20 titles. This marks a sharp drop from last year's six female filmmakers, who made up 29% of the competition slate. The festival's Horizons section, however, will feature a record-breaking 42% of films directed by women, with eight out of 19 titles.

stories.title: The Bottom Line
stories.summary: A panel of business leaders has chosen the winners and losers from the last 12 months in business, with Nicola Hodson, Deputy President of techUK, among the guests on the BBC's annual awards show, The Bottom Line. The show, hosted by Evan Davis, features a review of the past year's business successes and failures. The judges' verdicts will be revealed in the broadcast.

stories.title: Venice Lesbian Drama ‘Big Little Things’ Boards Moebius Entertainment for Worldwide Sales
stories.summary: Michelle Zhou’s debut feature “Big Little Things,” a Hong Kong-U.S. co-production about identity and family expectations, has secured worldwide sales representation from Moebius Entertainment after premiering in the Horizons section of the 83rd Venice Film Festival. The film follows a woman returning to Harbin who faces a choice between her girlfriend and her family’s demands. Moebius will now pitch the project to international buyers and festivals following its earlier Golden Horse Film Project Promotion win in Taipei.

stories.title: Google hit with $1 billion fine for breaking EU antitrust rules
stories.summary: Google's parent company Alphabet has been fined €890 million, approximately $1 billion, by the European Union for breaking antitrust rules. The penalties stem from Google giving preferential treatment to its own services in search results and blocking developers from promoting alternative payment options. Alphabet has 60 days to make policy changes or face further periodic fines.

stories.title: Potter and Yee among favourites at World Triathlon London event
stories.summary: Olympic gold medallist Alex Yee and world-ranked Beth Potter headline the World Triathlon Championship Series in London this weekend. Both Britons compete at ExCeL London as the elite series reaches its mid-season climax. The finals take place in Pontevedra, Spain, in September.

stories.title: Islanders asked to 'track' not 'trample' Asian hornets
stories.summary: Guernsey authorities urge residents to report Asian hornet sightings to protect local biodiversity and honeybees. So far in 2024, 205 queens have been caught and nine primary nests destroyed. Islanders are asked to photograph and track hornets before submitting sightings to help locate and destroy nests.

stories.title: Newcastle agree £34m deal for Monaco's Bamba
stories.summary: Newcastle United have agreed a £34 million deal for Monaco’s 20-year-old midfielder Ibrahim Bamba. The transfer marks the club’s fourth signing of the summer as Eddie Howe continues to reshape the squad. Bamba is expected to complete his medical before joining the Magpies.

stories.title: Imax Second Quarter Earnings Rise as CEO Calls Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey’ a ‘Transformational Event’
stories.summary: Imax reported a 12% revenue rise to $102.8 million in Q2, with net income up 30% to $15.9 million. CEO credited Christopher Nolan’s ‘The Odyssey’ as a key driver, calling it a transformational event.

stories.title: Google now lets you sign in to your account using a selfie video
stories.summary: Google has introduced a new feature allowing users to sign in to their account using a selfie video, providing an alternative to traditional password and two-factor authentication methods. This feature requires users to capture a short video of their face, which is then compared to a stored reference video to confirm account ownership. Users can delete the saved selfie video at any time, but can also opt to share it for additional purposes.

stories.title: Metcalf out of England's Commonwealth netball squad
stories.summary: England’s netball captain Natalie Metcalf has been ruled out of the Commonwealth Games in Glasgow with a calf injury. The 33-year-old, who won gold in 2018, was replaced by Ella Clark and will miss England’s opening match against Northern Ireland on 25 July. England, ranked third in the world, now face Australia and five other nations in Pool A.

stories.title: A New Target for Ukraine’s Long-Range Attacks: Russia’s Version of Amazon
stories.summary: Kyiv is escalating its air war by striking commercial warehouses, trying to bring the conflict closer to home for ordinary Russians.

stories.title: Global Oil Price Nears $100 a Barrel as Conflict With Iran Deepens
stories.summary: The Houthis claimed to have attacked two ships in the Red Sea, and Iran said a third caught fire near the Strait of Hormuz.

stories.title: River levels approach all-time low as drought announcement looms
stories.summary: River levels in Wales are approaching all-time lows as drought conditions intensify. Natural Resources Wales is set to announce an environmental drought for parts of the country later today. The prolonged dry spell has already caused significant ecological damage and threatens agriculture.

stories.title: US signs landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia
stories.summary: The US has signed a landmark nuclear deal with Saudi Arabia, allowing the kingdom to enrich uranium on its soil to produce fuel at a US-built enrichment facility. This deal comes as tensions escalate between the US and Iran, with Saudi Arabia hosting several US bases targeted by Iranian attacks. The agreement is expected to be worth billions of dollars to US companies involved in the project.

stories.title: Google burning through cash with spiralling AI costs
stories.summary: Alphabet reported a record negative free cash flow of $5.9 billion in its latest quarter as AI infrastructure costs surged. The company now expects to spend up to $205 billion on AI this year, up from $190 billion, despite revenue hitting $119.8 billion, a 23% increase. Analysts note the shift reflects the massive investment race in AI technology.

stories.title: 'I am still alive': Indian activist on hunger strike for 26 days loses 11kg
stories.summary: Indian activist Lobsang Wangchuk has been on a hunger strike for 26 days, losing 11 kilograms, as protests against exam paper leaks continue to escalate in the country. The demonstrations have stirred public anger and posed a challenge for Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government, with Modi attempting to reach out to students through a recent statement on social media. The protests are expected to continue until their demands are met.

stories.title: A Peptide Showdown: F.D.A. Scientists May Clash With R.F.K.’s Agenda
stories.summary: Agency scientists are advising against allowing widespread use of several peptide injections. Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the health secretary, has said people should be free to experiment.

stories.title: Nuclear Energy Revival Puts Westinghouse in Prime Position
stories.summary: The company, which filed for bankruptcy protection in 2017, stands to benefit from growing support for nuclear power and President Trump’s deal with Saudi Arabia.

stories.title: Should Travelers Give Up on Summer in Western Europe Because of the Heat?
stories.summary: A favorite vacation option may be at risk because of rising temperatures. Even those sticking with their summer plans agree: More air conditioning would help.

stories.title: Alarm over hospital lung admissions for children in England’s poorest postcodes
stories.summary: Children living in England's poorest postcodes are 40% more likely to be admitted to hospital for respiratory infections, with 1,941 out of every 100,000 children in the most deprived areas being admitted in 2024/25. This alarming trend contrasts with the 1,380 admissions per 100,000 in the least deprived areas, resulting in a total of 82% more hospital admissions for respiratory infections. Experts are now raising concerns about the impact of air pollution on young people's health in these disadvantaged areas.

stories.title: 'Life-changing' MS drug to help people walk now on NHS in England
stories.summary: The NHS in England will now offer fampridine, the first drug to help some people with multiple sclerosis walk, to up to 5,000 patients annually. The treatment boosts nerve signals to improve mobility and independence. Clinical trials showed it helped about 40% of users walk faster and longer.
